---
title: "AI in Research: Harnessing the Strengths, Controlling the Weaknesses"
subtitle: "Tools and Techniques for Responsible AI Use in Research"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---


## The Future Is Already Here

Suppose you want to quickly put together a figure showing how Norway and other European countries compare on student learning and well-being — combining PISA academic achievement data with HBSC well-being data. Downloading the files, wrangling two large international datasets, constructing composite scores, and producing a labelled scatterplot would normally take an afternoon of coding. How quickly can this actually be done?

Here is a condensed version of the prompt[^1] given to an AI assistant with access to the web and code execution (the full prompt is in the footnote[^2]):

> - Download HBSC data and extract well-being, life satisfaction, and self-efficacy measures for 15-year-olds; compute a composite score per country.
> - Download the PISA 2022 student file from Zenodo; build a composite from reading, mathematics, and science; compute European country averages.
> - Plot a scatterplot of HBSC composite (x) vs. PISA composite (y); label all Nordic countries; highlight Norway.
> - Use R; save the script, figure, and all data to specified folders.


Here is the figure that came out:

![PISA 2022 vs. HBSC well-being composite by European country](figures/pisa_hbsc_composite_scatter.png)

Not bad for a single short prompt. The pipeline ran end to end — web scraping, data wrangling, composite scoring across two large international datasets, and a labelled scatterplot — with no manual coding. The AI searched the web, downloaded files, wrote and executed R code, read error messages, and corrected itself until the script ran cleanly.

The result isn't perfect. A more complete workflow would include explicit instructions on how to construct composite scores (e.g., z-score each variable before averaging), what axis labels to use, and what the figure should communicate. It would also specify what to do with missing data and how to handle countries that appear in one dataset but not the other.

The output scales with the quality of the instructions — that's the pattern. A short prompt produces a plausible first draft. A well-specified prompt, written by someone who knows what a good analysis looks like, produces something close to a finished product.

The PISA example used agentic tools. Most of what follows doesn't. Paste a set of rough meeting notes into any AI chatbot with the instruction "turn these into a structured analysis plan with hypotheses, outcomes, and decision rules" and you have a working SAP draft in minutes. The same principle applies across the research workflow — the tool does the mechanical work; you supply the expertise to evaluate what comes back.

This is not a demo. It's what current agentic AI systems[^3] can do. The question is no longer whether AI will change how we do research, but how to use it in ways that are actually reliable.

But powerful doesn't mean problem-free. The limitations matter, especially for researchers.

## Why LLMs Are Tricky for Researchers

The same architecture that makes large language models (LLMs)[^4] fluent also makes them unreliable in specific ways — and both properties stem from the same source.

### Regression to the Mode

LLMs are trained to predict the next most-likely token[^5] (word fragment) given everything that came before. That's what makes them fluent. It's also what makes them conservative.

Ask an LLM to summarise evidence on a contested topic and it will often produce a smooth, confident synthesis that papers over genuine scientific disagreement. Ask it to generate a novel hypothesis and it'll tend toward ideas that are already well-represented in the literature.

For researchers, regression to the mode is a subtle trap: the output *sounds* authoritative while being epistemically conservative. This is especially problematic when you're querying an LLM about a research topic and relying on its training data. The model will confidently reproduce the dominant view in the literature — including its biases, blind spots, and outdated consensus — without flagging minority findings, ongoing debates, or developments that postdate its training. It can look like a thorough synthesis while missing exactly the nuance that matters.

### Hallucination

Because LLMs are trained to produce plausible continuations rather than factually grounded ones, they sometimes generate content that is fluent but false. The canonical problem is fabricated citations — papers that don't exist, authors who didn't write them, DOIs that resolve to unrelated content. But hallucination extends to statistics, dates, methodology descriptions, and any claim that requires retrieving a specific fact from memory rather than reasoning from the prompt.

For researchers, this isn't merely embarrassing — it's a validity threat. A results section that invents numbers, or a literature review that cites phantom papers, can compromise research integrity. The good news is that there are practical ways to make LLM use safer, and most of them don't require much effort.



## A Framework for Responsible Use

Both problems have practical fixes — three levers, in increasing order of effort:

- **Lever 1: Custom instructions, extended reasoning, and good prompts** — set how the model behaves in every conversation.
- **Lever 2: Custom chatbots** — purpose-built specialists with domain expertise baked in.
- **Lever 3: Ground the model in your own documents** — so answers draw from your papers, not the model's training memory.

What follows works through each lever, from the simplest to the most involved. If you want the quick version, skip ahead to [Getting Started](#getting-started).


::: {.callout-warning}
## Data Privacy: What Not to Upload

**Raw research data must not be uploaded.** This applies to participant-level data, health records, administrative data, and any dataset with privacy implications — regardless of pseudonymisation. The exception is data you downloaded from a public source or would share openly anyway (the PISA and HBSC files above fall into this category).

Pre-publication results are a different matter. Sharing a table of means or a draft results section carries no privacy risk, and using an LLM to help draft or check such text is generally fine. ^[What does require care is training data. Standard consumer-facing LLM services may use your conversations to improve their models unless you opt out or use a version that excludes this. For tasks involving sensitive materials — confidential grant applications, internal project documents, unpublished methods — use a service that contractually excludes your prompts from training data. Enterprise versions of Microsoft Copilot (Microsoft 365) and the API-based tiers of Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini typically provide this guarantee; verify the terms for the specific service before proceeding.]

Follow FHI's guidelines on AI use. If you're uncertain whether a particular use is permitted, check with your data manager or privacy officer before proceeding.
:::


## Lever 1: Custom Instructions

*~5 min · beginner · Every conversation starts better-behaved*

Every major LLM interface now lets you set custom instructions[^8] — instructions that apply to every conversation. It's the easiest, highest-leverage thing a researcher can do.

Here's a set of custom instructions designed for researchers:

```
- Say "I don't know" rather than guessing; never fabricate citations or statistics
- Use web search and tools to verify facts before stating them
- Cite primary literature; prefer peer-reviewed sources over summaries
- Push back on flawed reasoning or unsupported claims
- Think step by step and check your reasoning before answering
- Read papers critically: flag when conclusions outrun the evidence, when study designs cannot support causal claims, or when limitations are understated
- Be concise; do not pad responses or ask unnecessary follow-up questions
- Do not suggest next steps or offer further help unless asked
```

These instructions tackle hallucination directly ("never fabricate citations"), counter the tendency toward overconfident synthesis ("push back on flawed reasoning"), and cut noise ("do not pad responses"). A model following these behaves quite differently from the default.

### Setting custom instructions in Microsoft Copilot

Microsoft Copilot (available at [copilot.microsoft.com](https://copilot.microsoft.com) and integrated into Microsoft 365) supports custom instructions through its settings.

**Step-by-step:**

1. Open Microsoft Copilot 365.
2. Click the three dots beside your **profile icon** and select **Settings** (or the gear icon).
3. Click on **Personalization** in the left side bar and then click on **Revise instructions** (under **Custom instructions**) on the right side.
4. In the text field, paste your custom instructions. You can include multiple bullet points; the model will apply them persistently.
5. Save your settings. From this point on, every new Copilot conversation will start with these instructions already in effect.

<video autoplay loop muted playsinline width="50%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
  <source src="figures/CustomInstructions.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>

> **Tip:** Custom instructions in Copilot apply across sessions but not across all Microsoft 365 entry points (e.g., Copilot in Word may not inherit browser-based instructions). Check the specific integration you are using.

### Extended Reasoning

Most major chatbots now offer an extended reasoning mode — variously labelled *thinking*, *deep think*, *extended thinking*, or *deep research* depending on the provider. When it's on, the model works through a problem step by step before generating its response, rather than jumping to the most statistically likely continuation.

Some services, including Microsoft Copilot 365, offer an *auto* setting where the model decides how much reasoning to apply. That's fine for everyday tasks. For analytically demanding work, explicitly select a higher reasoning setting — leaving it to the model means it may under-invest in reasoning exactly when that matters most.

This is where extended reasoning pays off: evaluating a causal argument, working through a statistical model specification, critiquing a study design, deriving a testable hypothesis from a theoretical claim. These are exactly the tasks where standard LLMs tend to produce confident-sounding but shallow answers. Extended reasoning doesn't completely eliminate it, but substantially reduces it.

It's a single toggle. Enable it by default for anything methodologically demanding; turn it off for simple drafting tasks where speed matters more.

<video autoplay loop muted playsinline width="50%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
  <source src="figures/Thinking.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>

### Writing Good Prompts

Custom instructions shape how the model behaves across all conversations; a good prompt shapes how it behaves for a specific task. Both matter.

What distinguishes a good prompt from a weak one:

- **State the goal, not the procedure.** Describe what a correct output looks like, not the steps you assume the model should follow. The model will often find a better path than the one you'd have prescribed.
- **Provide the relevant context.** Include the research question, the data structure, the relevant constraints, and any domain knowledge the model would otherwise lack. An agent reasons from what's in the context window[^6]; context you omit is context it can't use.
- **Define the output format explicitly.** Specify length, structure, and medium: a table, a prose paragraph, executable R code, a bulleted list with citations. Vague requests produce variable outputs.
- **Flag what to avoid.** If there are common errors, off-target interpretations, or things you already know don't apply, say so.
- **For complex tasks, break the task into explicit steps.** A single long prompt asking for multiple things tends to produce uneven output.

The same research topic, two very different prompts — and two very different outputs:

<table style="font-size: 0.8em; width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; margin: 1em 0;">
<colgroup>
  <col style="width: 25%">
  <col style="width: 75%">
</colgroup>
<thead>
<tr style="border-bottom: 2px solid #dee2e6;">
  <th style="text-align: left; padding: 0.5em 1em 0.5em 0; font-weight: 600;">Weak prompt</th>
  <th style="text-align: left; padding: 0.5em 0 0.5em 1em; font-weight: 600;">Stronger prompt</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td style="vertical-align: top; padding: 0.75em 1em 0.75em 0;">
<pre style="margin: 0; white-space: pre-wrap; background: #f8f9fa; padding: 0.6em 0.75em; border-radius: 4px; font-size: inherit; border: 1px solid #e9ecef;">Summarise the research on the effects of social media use on mental health.</pre>
</td>
<td style="vertical-align: top; padding: 0.75em 0 0.75em 1em;">
<pre style="margin: 0; white-space: pre-wrap; background: #f8f9fa; padding: 0.6em 0.75em; border-radius: 4px; font-size: inherit; border: 1px solid #e9ecef;">I am reviewing evidence on the effects of social media use on mental health in adolescents (ages 13–18), with a focus on depression and anxiety outcomes.
Please do the following:
1. Summarise what the evidence shows, distinguishing clearly between correlational/cross-sectional studies and studies that support stronger causal claims (longitudinal designs, natural experiments, randomised trials).
2. Flag where the literature is genuinely contested — effect sizes, direction of causality, whether effects differ by platform, content type, sex, or age.
3. Note the key methodological weaknesses in this literature (e.g., self-reported outcomes, reverse causality, confounding, publication bias).
4. Identify 3–5 specific papers I should read, with enough detail that I can verify they exist (authors, year, journal). Do not invent citations.
Do not smooth over disagreements. If the evidence is weak or mixed, say so.
Format: structured prose with a short summary table at the end (study type,
finding, strength of evidence).</pre>
</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

The weak prompt will produce a smooth, confident overview that papers over genuine scientific disagreement — exactly the regression-to-the-mode problem described above. The model will reproduce the dominant narrative in the literature, blend correlational and experimental evidence without distinguishing them, and present contested findings as settled. It will not tell you this is what it's doing.

The difference is not length for its own sake. Every added element is doing work: the population specification prevents the model from blending adult and adolescent evidence; the causal inference instruction forces it to treat a cross-sectional correlation differently from an experiment; the "do not smooth over disagreements" instruction directly counteracts regression to the mode; the citation instruction with a verification requirement reduces hallucination risk. The output format request means you get something you can actually use rather than a wall of prose to restructure yourself.

> **Tip:** Paste a weak first draft of a prompt and ask the model to identify what's underspecified. It'll often surface the gaps you missed. Answer those questions, then ask the model to write an improved prompt incorporating your answers. Two or three exchanges usually produces something substantially better than most people write from scratch.

> **Tip:** Start a new chat for each new question or task. Long conversations that span multiple topics accumulate context that can pull the model off-target — earlier exchanges linger in the context window and can skew later responses. A fresh chat keeps the context focused.

## Lever 2: Custom Chatbots

*~15 min · beginner · Reusable specialist available any time*

Think of a custom chatbot as a specialist you talk to — it gives expert advice within a conversation. An agent, by contrast, is a specialist that does work for you: editing files, running code, taking actions on your behalf. For most research tasks, the chatbot form is what you need.

Custom instructions give you a better-behaved generalist assistant. Custom chatbots go further: instead of starting each task in the default chat, you open a purpose-built chatbot — one that already has domain expertise, methodological guardrails, and a curated knowledge base baked in. The result is a reusable specialist you can return to across projects and share with colleagues, without re-specifying your requirements each time.

### What a custom chatbot specification looks like

A chatbot specification has four parts:

- **Name and description:** a short, specific label that makes the chatbot's purpose immediately clear — both for your own reference and for colleagues you share it with (e.g., "Methods Consultant — Epidemiology" / "Causal inference and study design advisor for population health research")
- **Instructions:** what the chatbot should and shouldn't do, how it should reason, what it should ask before answering
- **Knowledge:** files you upload to the chatbot — PDFs of methods papers, textbooks, reporting checklists, your institution's guidelines — stored with the agent and drawn on when generating responses. When you ask a question, the chatbot searches this uploaded material and uses the relevant passages to inform its answer, rather than relying solely on what the underlying model learned during training.
- **Tools:** web search, code execution, file access (depending on platform)

A full worked example — the *Methods Consultant — Epidemiology* specification — together with a table of all available chatbots is in the [Appendix](#appendix-chatbot-example-and-overview).

### Setting up custom chatbots and agents in Microsoft Copilot

Microsoft Copilot supports custom configurations (called **Copilot Agents** in Copilot 365) that embed persistent instructions, knowledge, and tools. For most research use cases, a **custom chatbot** is what you need: a named assistant with fixed instructions and an uploaded knowledge base that you interact with conversationally. This becomes a true **agent**[^9] when you also grant it the ability to take actions — generating and editing Word or PowerPoint files, sending emails, querying SharePoint. For a methods consultant or copy editor, the chatbot form is usually enough. The agent form becomes useful when you want the AI to produce a draft document, not just advise on its content.

**Step-by-step (Copilot Studio / Microsoft 365 Copilot):**

1. Open MS Copilot 365.
2. On the left side bar, click on **New agent**. This will open the agent creation field on the right side.
3. Give the agent a **name** and a short **description** (e.g., "Methods Consultant — Epidemiology").
4. In the **Instructions** field, paste the instructions from the chatbot specification. Be specific: the more precise the instructions, the more reliably the agent will behave.
5. Under **Knowledge**, click **Add knowledge** and upload relevant documents — PDFs of methods papers, reporting checklists, your project's analysis plan.
6. Configure **Actions** (tools) as needed: web search, file access, or code interpreter if available in your tenant.
7. Click **Create** to make the agent available.

<video autoplay loop muted playsinline width="50%" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
  <source src="figures/CustomChatbot.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>

> **Tip:** Copilot agents can be scoped to your organisation, shared with a team, or kept private. For a methods consultant that references your institution's statistical guidelines, keeping it within your organisation makes sense.

> **Tip:** For best results, upload Markdown rather than raw PDFs — PDF text extraction is often poor and can degrade the quality of answers. See [Document Quality for Knowledge Bases](#appendix-document-quality) in the Appendix.


## Lever 3: Grounding the Model in Your Own Documents

*~10–30 min · beginner · Model answers from your papers, not its training memory*

Custom chatbots with a fixed knowledge base are powerful, but they require you to anticipate which documents you need. RAG — Retrieval Augmented Generation — systems go further: they maintain a searchable knowledge base and retrieve relevant passages *at query time* using semantic search.[^10]

When you ask a question, the system retrieves the passages most relevant to your query and places them in the model's context window before it generates its answer — so the model works from your documents, not its training memory. The practical effect is direct: instead of hallucinating a citation from memory, the model is constrained to reason from text you supplied and can verify.

### Practical RAG tools for researchers

**[NotebookLM](https://notebooklm.google.com/)** (Google) is the most accessible RAG tool for researchers — and it's free. You upload your sources — PDFs, Markdown, Google Docs, web pages — and NotebookLM creates a searchable notebook grounded in those sources. Answers include citations to the specific passage used, so you can verify them directly. It's a general-purpose tool, though, and lacks the research-specific features that make systematic literature work faster.

Use it for:

- Literature synthesis from a curated set of papers
- Drafting introductions or discussion sections grounded in your reference list
- Answering specific questions about uploaded documents, which can be papers, textbooks, or documentations for software like R-packages

**[Elicit](https://elicit.com/)** is purpose-built for research. It searches a large academic database and extracts structured information — outcomes, population, methods, effect sizes — from papers in a way that's directly useful for systematic reviews and evidence synthesis. The trade-off is cost and setup: to use Elicit effectively, you need one of the better paid plans (around 4,500 NOK/year), and cheaper accounts analyse only a small number of full texts per month. Even with a more expensive plan, getting full-text reasoning often means uploading PDFs yourself. That extra step is worth it for systematic review work, but Elicit rewards deliberate setup rather than casual use.

**In both cases:** documents converted with `pdfParser` before upload will give the RAG system more accurate, structured text to index.

The three levers above require no programming skills. For researchers comfortable with R or working in a coding environment, the Appendix describes two additional approaches: programmatic number verification with `tblscribe`, and agentic systems for automating full analysis pipelines.

## Putting It Together: A Practical Workflow

The table below maps a typical research workflow — from grant writing through to manuscript editing — to the AI tools described in this post. It is not a prescription: most researchers will find AI useful at some stages and not others, and starting with one or two tasks where the benefit is obvious is more sustainable than trying to integrate AI everywhere at once. The point is that for each major stage there is either a dedicated tool or a custom chatbot that is straightforward to set up, and they are designed to work together — the SAP produced by the SAP / Prereg Drafter becomes the document the Data Analyst — R chatbot works from, which in turn produces the output tables the Results Writer uses to plan and draft the results section.

<div style="font-size: 0.8em;">
| Task | Tool | Verification | Watch out for |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grant writing and review | Grant Reviewer chatbot | Your knowledge of funder criteria | Feedback reflects the knowledge base; keep it current with funder priorities |
| Literature review and synthesis | NotebookLM or Elicit^A^ | Citation checking against sources | Retrieval is imperfect; not all relevant passages will surface |
| Methods and study design | Methods Consultant chatbot | Your domain expertise | May miss field-specific norms not covered in the knowledge base |
| Analysis planning | SAP / Prereg Drafter chatbot | Methods consultant review | Formalises what was agreed; does not substitute for agreeing it first |
| Data preprocessing and analysis | Data Analyst — R chatbot | Run the code; inspect outputs | Code may run correctly but implement the wrong analysis |
| Describing results | Results Writer chatbot or `tblscribe`^B^ | Programmatic number check; expert review of framing | Numbers are verified; interpretation still needs expert review |
| Manuscript editing | Copy Editor — English chatbot | Read the diff carefully | Style changes can inadvertently alter meaning |
| Plain-language editing (Norwegian) | Copy Editor — Enkelt Språk chatbot | Read the diff carefully | Register simplification can lose precision; verify key claims remain accurate |

*Support tools (both require R): ^A^[`pdfParser`](https://github.com/gbiele/pdfParser) converts PDFs to clean Markdown before upload. ^B^[`tblscribe`](#appendix-tblscribe) programmatically verifies numbers in AI-generated text.*
</div>

Instructions shape model behaviour, context windows filled with reliable information ground model reasoning, and verification — whether programmatic or expert — catches what slips through.

## Getting Started

Most of the benefit described in this post comes from two steps that together take under an 30 minutes.

**Step 1: Set up custom instructions.** Copy the example from the Lever 1 section, paste it into Copilot's settings (or whichever tool you use), and adjust a line or two to fit your field. Done. Every conversation from this point starts with a model that's more careful, more honest about uncertainty, and less likely to pad its answers. 

**Step 2: Create one to three custom chatbots.** Think about the research tasks you turn to an LLM for most often — literature questions, methods advice, writing feedback, code help. Pick one to three. Then describe what you need to an LLM and ask it to write a custom chatbot specification for that task. Paste the result into Copilot Studio as a new agent, and you have a purpose-built assistant ready to use. It doesn't need to be perfect — it needs to be good enough to start. You'll notice quickly when it falls short, and a short conversation with the LLM is usually enough to improve the instructions.

The chatbots in the `chatbots/` directory are ready-made starting points if you want to skip even that first step. Take one that fits a task you already do, adjust the instructions to match your field and habits, and you're done.

With time, you can deepen the knowledge base, tighten the instructions, and add more chatbots as you find new uses. But the initial payoff — better answers, fewer hallucinations, a specialist available any time — comes quickly and with very little effort.

One practical note on tool selection: model capabilities change fast. When choosing between services, ask a model with web search to look up recent benchmarks rather than relying on what it knows from training — the landscape moves faster than any training dataset.

## Conclusion

The tools described here don't change what good research looks like — they change how much of the mechanical work you have to do yourself. Custom instructions and chatbots handle repetitive framing tasks: drafting, checking, reformatting. RAG systems make it harder to cite a paper you haven't read. Agentic systems collapse multi-step pipelines from an afternoon to a prompt.

What that frees up is the work that still requires a researcher: identifying the right question, judging whether a result is plausible, deciding what the evidence actually implies. LLMs are amplifiers — they multiply the competences you already have. Used carelessly, they scale your weaknesses just as readily. Used well, they give you back the time to focus on the part of the work that no model can replace.

Start with [Step 1 in Getting Started](#getting-started). Five minutes of setup changes every conversation that follows.

*The chatbot specifications referenced in this post are available in the `chatbots/` directory. The `pdfParser` and `tblscribe` R packages are available on GitHub.*

## Appendix

### Chatbot Example and Overview {#appendix-chatbot-example-and-overview}

#### Methods Consultant — Epidemiology: Full Specification

**Instructions** 
(One example per theme (full instructions available in the [`chatbots/` directory](chatbots/methods_consultant_epidemiology.md))):

```
- **Role**: You are an expert in epidemiological methods and causal inference for population health research.
- **Clarifying before answering**: Ask clarifying questions about the research question, target population, exposure, outcome, time horizon, and available data before recommending methods.
- **Causal reasoning with DAGs**: For causal questions, require the researcher to draw or describe a DAG before recommending adjustment sets; explain the difference between confounders, mediators, and colliders.
- **Estimand specification**: Before recommending a study design, require the researcher to state the target estimand precisely — what quantity, in what population, under what exposure contrast, at what time horizon.
- **Effect measures**: When the target estimand is a marginal measure, recommend risk ratios or risk differences (Poisson with robust SEs, log-binomial, marginal standardization) rather than odds ratios.
- **Standard errors**: Match the SE estimator to the data structure — cluster-robust SEs for clustered or multilevel data; explain when classical SEs mislead.
- **p-value interpretation**: Correct p-value misinterpretations when they occur; always require effect measures with confidence intervals, not p-values alone.
- **Reproducible science**: Require that all analyses are fully scripted from raw data to final results — every cleaning step, model, table, and figure must be re-runnable without manual intervention.
- **Reporting guidelines**: Reference STROBE, RECORD, and other reporting guidelines appropriate to the study design.
- **Intellectual honesty**: Say "I don't know" or "consult a methods expert" when questions exceed your expertise; never fabricate citations.
```

**Knowledge base (excerpt):**

These are the actual documents (PDFs or better markdown files, see the [section on document quality](#appendix-document-quality)) uploaded to the custom chatbot. When you ask a question, the agent uses the relevant passages in its response — so it can cite and reason from the specific content of these papers rather than from the model's general training:

- Hernán & Robins (2006). Estimating causal effects from epidemiological data.
- Greenland, Pearl & Robins (1999). Causal diagrams for epidemiologic research.
- Hernán & Robins (2016). Using big data to emulate a target trial.
- STROBE and RECORD reporting checklists

The effect is substantial. Instead of offering a generic answer to "should I adjust for X in my regression?", this chatbot will ask about your DAG, identify whether X is a confounder, mediator, or collider, and explain the consequences of conditioning on each — just as a good methodologist would.

#### All Available Chatbots

The chatbots listed below were generated with AI assistance and reviewed once. Treat them as starting points: read the instructions, test them on a real task, and adjust where they fall short for your field or workflow.

| Chatbot | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Grant Reviewer | Critical review against funder criteria (RCN, ERC, NIH) |
| Literature Synthesizer | Structured extraction and synthesis from uploaded PDFs with strict traceability — study design, estimand, findings, limitations in comparable tables |
| Narrative Literature Reviewer | Critical narrative reviews from uploaded sources, foregrounding contradictions, ongoing debates, and methodological quality |
| Methods Consultant | General statistics and study design for social/health sciences |
| Methods Consultant — Psychology | Psychology-specific, with emphasis on open science and lessons from the replication crisis |
| Methods Consultant — Epidemiology | Causal inference, DAGs, and epidemiological study designs |
| Methods Consultant — Econometrics | Causal identification strategies: IV, DiD, RDD, program evaluation |
| SAP / Prereg Drafter | Formalises an agreed analysis plan into a structured SAP or OSF pre-registration with hypotheses, estimands, and decision rules |
| Data Analyst — R | Interactive step-by-step coding assistant for R pipelines — works through data preprocessing and analysis using data.table, ggplot2, and the targets framework; asks for a SAP before any analysis code |
| Results Writer | Interactive assistant for results sections: helps outline what to report, then gives structured feedback (completeness, accuracy, SAP fidelity, style) on drafted text |
| Copy Editor — English | Academic copy editing for English manuscripts — clarity, grammar, and style while preserving scientific meaning |
| Copy Editor — Enkelt Språk | Plain-language rewriting in Norwegian for general or non-specialist audiences |
| Code Writer | General-purpose reproducible coding assistant for R or Python — use when the interactive Data Analyst — R workflow is not needed or when working in Python |

These chatbot specifications were largely generated with AI assistance and should be treated as starting points, not finished products. Review the instructions, adjust them to your field and workflow, and refine them as you discover where the defaults fall short.

### Document Quality for Knowledge Bases {#appendix-document-quality}

The knowledge base in a custom chatbot or RAG system is only as good as the text the platform can actually extract from it. When you upload a PDF to Copilot Studio, NotebookLM, or a similar platform, the platform extracts the text automatically before indexing it. This extraction is often poor: equations become garbled, tables lose their structure, and multi-column layouts produce jumbled, out-of-order text. The chatbot then reasons from this degraded version — not from the paper as you read it.

A better approach is to upload a clean **Markdown** version instead of the raw PDF. Markdown is plain text with simple formatting that platforms can index accurately, without any lossy extraction step.

[`pdfParser`](https://github.com/gbiele/pdfParser) is an R package that converts PDFs to semantic Markdown using [Docling](https://github.com/DS4SD/docling) for layout analysis and body text, and Google Gemini for table and figure extraction. The result preserves the semantic content of a paper — tables with correct cell alignment, figure captions, and section hierarchy — in a format that chatbot platforms can index cleanly. It includes a `pdf_dir_to_markdown_docling()` function that processes all PDFs in a folder in one step, making it straightforward to prepare an entire knowledge base at once.

For knowledge-base documents you return to repeatedly — core methods papers, reporting checklists, textbooks — converting them once with `pdfParser` and uploading the Markdown versions is worth the extra step.

### Programmatic Verification with tblscribe {#appendix-tblscribe}

One of the most practically useful applications of LLMs in research is generating text from statistical results — drafting results sections, municipality-specific reports, or subgroup summaries from tabular output. The risk is numerical hallucination: an LLM given a results table may misread a value, transpose digits, or confabulate a statistic.

At the Norwegian Institute of Public Health (FHI), this problem is concrete: FHI produces reports for hundreds of municipalities, schools, and population subgroups. Writing subgroup-specific text by hand doesn't scale; LLM-generated text is fast but can introduce numerical errors.

[`tblscribe`](https://github.com/gbiele/tblscribe) is an R package that addresses this directly. It:

1. Formats a data frame or statistical table into a prompt-safe representation
2. Sends it to an LLM (OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google) with a prompt template
3. **Verifies the generated text** by checking that every numeric token in the prose matches the source table within specified tolerances — deterministically, without re-querying the model

You pass a data frame and a prompt to `tblscribe`'s main function; it sends both to the LLM, receives the generated prose, and then checks every number in that prose against the source table. If there's a mismatch — the model writes 0.32 when the table says 0.31 — `tblscribe` flags it, optionally retries with corrective feedback, and can prepend a warning to the output so downstream users know verification failed.

This is what makes AI-assisted text generation safe at scale: the LLM generates the prose, deterministic code verifies the numbers, and humans review flagged outputs. Neither step alone is sufficient.


### Agentic Systems {#appendix-agentic-systems}

An agentic AI system goes beyond answering questions in a chat window. It can use tools — web search, code execution, file reading and writing — and execute sequences of actions autonomously, correcting itself when something fails and continuing until the task is done. The PISA/HBSC figure at the top of this post illustrates what this looks like in practice: given the download URLs and a description of the desired output, the agent downloaded two international datasets, wrote R code to clean and merge them, built composite scores, produced a labelled scatterplot, fixed the errors that arose, and saved the figure and script — all from a single prompt, without manual intervention at any step.

![An overview of the Cursor agentic coding environment, showing its AI-native editor interface with inline code generation and debugging capabilities.](figures/Cursor.png)

### Some Available Tools

The main agentic coding environments used in research settings are [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/download) (Anthropic), which is particularly strong for R and Python data pipelines and can be used directly in a terminal or IDE; [Cursor](https://www.cursor.com), an AI-native code editor that writes, runs, and debugs code inline; [GitHub Copilot Workspace](https://githubnext.com/projects/copilot-workspace), which works within GitHub's ecosystem; and Microsoft Copilot with Code Interpreter, available in some Microsoft 365 configurations. All of them support the same core workflow: describe the task, let the agent write and run the code, review the output. If you already use Visual Studio Code, the easiest entry point is to install the [Claude Code extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=anthropic.claude-code) directly in the editor — you get the full agentic functionality without leaving the environment you already work in.

### What Agentic Systems Are Best At

Agentic systems are strongest at tasks that are well-defined, repetitive, and verifiable. Automating a data pipeline — download, clean, derive variables, fit models, produce tables and figures — is exactly the kind of work they handle well. They translate a verbal description of an analysis into executable code, iterate until it runs, and produce outputs that can be inspected directly. The more precisely the task is specified, the closer the result is to what a careful analyst would produce.

### Agentic Systems as Expert Colleagues

With the right context loaded, an agentic system can act as something closer to a knowledgeable sparring partner than a code generator. If you provide the analysis plan, describe the data structure, and upload relevant background literature — the methods papers that define the estimand, the reporting guidelines for your field, the preregistration document — the agent can review model specifications and flag mismatches with the stated research question, propose alternative analytical approaches grounded in the uploaded methods papers, and catch common implementation errors before they propagate. The key is that the agent reasons from what is in the context window. An agent given only the data and a vague instruction will produce something plausible; an agent given the analysis plan, the relevant methods papers, and explicit criteria for what a correct implementation looks like will produce something that can be evaluated against a standard. The quality of the context you provide directly determines the quality of the review you get back.

To make this work well in practice, it helps to add *skills* to a project. Most agentic tools support some version of this idea: in Claude Code they are markdown files in `.claude/commands/`; in Cursor they are called Rules and live in `.cursor/rules/` as `.mdc` files with frontmatter that controls when they activate. In both cases, a skill or rule defines a set of instructions for a specific task — "review an analysis plan against the preregistration", "check R code for targets pipeline conventions" — and the agent loads them automatically when they are relevant, without being told explicitly.[^11] For a research project, a small library of skills — one for analysis plan review, one for results reporting, one for code style — means that domain-specific standards are applied consistently without having to repeat them in every prompt.

### What They're Still Not Good At

Even with rich context, agentic systems fall short in ways that matter for research. They tend not to generate genuinely novel scientific ideas — regression to the mode means output gravitates toward what is already common in the literature. An experienced researcher notices immediately when a result is implausible; an agent without that accumulated background knowledge cannot. Translating a theoretical prediction into a precise, testable statistical claim requires conceptual reasoning that LLMs handle poorly without very explicit scaffolding. And agents work with what is in front of them: they will not flag that a critical confound was never measured, that a sensitivity analysis is missing from the plan, or that the discussion ignores a rival explanation. LLMs multiply the competences you already have — they do not supply new ones. The value of agentic systems scales with your ability to evaluate what they produce.

[^1]: A **prompt** is the text input you give to an AI model — your question, instruction, or task description. The quality of the prompt strongly influences the quality of the output.

[^2]: Full prompt: "Download hsbc data here: https://data-browser.hbsc.org/wp-content/uploads/csvs/data.csv and meta data here https://data-browser.hbsc.org/wp-content/uploads/csvs/metadata.csv. Then extract data for 15 year olds — who-5-well-being-index, life satisfaction, self efficacy (2 measures) — from the data and calculate a composite score for each country. Next go to https://zenodo.org/records/13382904 and download the PISA 2022 student file. Build a composite score from reading, mathematics and science, and compute the average for each European country. Finally, plot a scatterplot with the composite HBSC score on the x-axis and the composite PISA score on the y-axis. Label all the Nordic countries and highlight Norway and save the figure as figures/scatter_pisa_hsbc.png. Use R to do this. Document all steps in a script, which you save as scripts/pisa_hbsc_scatter. Save all data in the data folder."

[^3]: An **agentic AI system** can autonomously plan and execute a sequence of actions — web searches, code execution, file reading and writing — to complete a complex multi-step task, rather than just responding to a single query.

[^4]: **Large language models (LLMs)** are AI models trained on vast amounts of text to predict and generate language. They power tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot.

[^5]: A **token** is the basic unit an LLM processes — roughly a word or word fragment. On average, one English word is about 1.3 tokens. LLMs don't read full words; they read sequences of tokens.

[^6]: The **context window** is the total amount of text an LLM can process at once — your prompt, any uploaded documents, and the conversation history. Information outside the context window is invisible to the model. Modern LLMs have large context windows (hundreds of thousands of tokens), but there are still limits, and what's in the window matters for answer quality.

[^8]: A **system prompt** is a set of instructions provided to the model before the conversation begins. It's always active but not directly visible in the chat. Custom instructions in Copilot, ChatGPT, and Claude all work by setting a system prompt.

[^9]: In the Microsoft Copilot context, an **agent** is a custom chatbot that can also take actions — creating or editing documents, sending emails, querying SharePoint — not just generate text in a conversation.

[^10]: Technically: your question is converted to a **vector embedding** — a numerical representation of its meaning — and matched against embeddings of document chunks. Passages with the highest semantic similarity are retrieved and inserted into the context window before the model answers. This means the model works from: "Given these specific passages, answer this question," rather than from training data alone.

[^11]: Unlike a custom chatbot, a skill is not a separate chat interface: it runs inside the agentic system, with full access to all project files and tools. The comparison to custom chatbots (described in the preceding appendix section) is useful here: a chatbot gives a specialist persona for a focused conversation; a skill gives the agentic system a standing instruction it can call on mid-task, combined with everything it already knows about your files and code.
